naming_inspiration.description: Named for his maternal uncle Charles Ogilvie Cannon (HD2798), his mother Antoinette's brother who died aged 23.
Named for his maternal uncle Charles Ogilvie Cannon (HD2798), his mother Antoinette's brother who died aged 23.

narrative_blocks.header: Left for Colorado Territory before statehood, age twenty-two
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Cannon Betts left Norwalk for the Colorado Territory around 1875 — before Colorado achieved statehood on August 1, 1876 — at roughly twenty-two years old, settling in Pueblo where he married Carrie Mallory in 1878. His family crossed three states: born in Connecticut, raised in Colorado, with an infant son dying in Texas while visiting Carrie's Mallory family connections in Corpus Christi.

burial.plot_notes: FindAGrave #100205188. Mountain View Cemetery Pueblo CO.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his uncle Thaddeus Clark Betts (HD4405, 1852-1877), who died five years before his birth — who was himself named for his grandfather Senator Thaddeus Betts. Three Thaddeus Betts across three generations.

narrative_blocks.body: Thaddeus Betts spent most of his life in Pueblo, Colorado as an automobile salesman — but never relinquished his membership in St. John's Lodge AF&AM in Norwalk, Connecticut, retaining it for more than fifty years from across the continent. His obituary in a Norwalk newspaper called him 'a former Norwalker,' anchoring his identity to the Connecticut town where his great-grandfather Senator Thaddeus Betts had served in the U.S. Senate.

burial.plot_notes: FindAGrave #233108826. Originally New Bayview Cemetery Corpus Christi; moved to Rose Hill Memorial Park 1914.
FindAGrave #139501372. Pine Ridge Cemetery Saranac Lake NY.

narrative_blocks.header: Pioneer therapist who died at Saranac Lake, thirty-eight
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Bissell Betts married Col. Edward Packard, a physician who would receive the Legion of Merit for commanding 6,500-bed military hospitals in WWII. His 1968 obituary described her as 'one of the earliest occupational therapists' — she died in 1926 at thirty-eight in Saranac Lake, New York, the famous tuberculosis sanatorium town where many sought or provided care during this era.

narrative_blocks.header: Born Wilton; survived her husband twenty-two years
narrative_blocks.body: Katharine White Betts was born in Wilton in 1872, married George Frederick Betts in 1905, and outlived him by twenty-two years — dying at ninety-one in Newport News, Virginia in 1964. Two of her three children predeceased her: Margaret, who died in infancy in 1907, and John Bissell, who died at twenty in 1928.

institutions.institution_blurb: Mt. Holyoke College class of 1931; lifelong member First Congregational Church on the Green, Norwalk.

narrative_blocks.header: Born and died Norwalk; ninety-eight years
narrative_blocks.body: Phyllis Jackson Hall was born in Norwalk on April 12, 1910, graduated from Mt. Holyoke College in 1931, married Howard Fales Hall on October 8, 1932, and died in Norwalk on October 29, 2008 at ninety-eight. She never left her hometown, remaining an active member of the First Congregational Church on the Green, the Girl Scouts, and the AAUW throughout her long Norwalk life.

burial.plot_notes: FindAGrave. Norwalk Union Cemetery CT. Also cenotaph at Congressional Cemetery Washington DC.

notable.notable_blurb: Senator who died in office; President attended funeral

education.notes: Yale College graduate 1807. Admitted to Connecticut bar 1810.

career.notes: Served from March 4, 1839 until death April 7, 1840. Died in office Washington DC.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College 1807; later twice Lt. Governor and US Senator from Connecticut.

cross_connections.display_label: Served with Benjamin Isaacs in CT House, May 1815

narrative_blocks.header: Funeral at US Capitol; President Van Buren attended
narrative_blocks.body: Thaddeus Betts died in Washington on April 7, 1840, just over a year into his Senate term as Connecticut's Whig senator. His funeral took place at the US Capitol with Congressional chaplains officiating and President Martin Van Buren in attendance — a remarkable honor for a lawyer from Norwalk who had once served in the Connecticut House alongside Benjamin Isaacs.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1807; first office alongside his future cousin-in-law
narrative_blocks.body: Betts graduated Yale in 1807, was admitted to the bar in 1810, and returned to Norwalk to practice — the town where his future wife Antoinette Cannon's family had descended from Rev. Thomas Hooker through six generations. His first CT House term in May 1815 came while serving alongside Benjamin Isaacs, Antoinette's first cousin through their shared Saint John ancestry.

notable.notable_blurb: Bridgeport founder, physician, turnpike company founder

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founder of the Borough of Bridgeport
narrative_blocks.detail: His estate was distributed in 1805 to his widow Sarah and their children. A note in the research suggests a John Cannon (possibly his son) founded the Bridgeport Bank in 1806 and served as its president from 1823 until 1827 — the probate timeline makes it likely this was the son John Jr. (HD2793) rather than the physician himself. In the 1790 US Census, one enslaved person was enumerated in his household. His daughter Antoinette married U.S. Senator Thaddeus Betts; his daughter Sarah married back into the St. John family. (St. John Genealogy; Bridgeport historical records)
narrative_blocks.body: John Cannon Jr. moved from Norwalk to Bridgeport (then called Newfield) around 1790. He was involved in founding the Newfield Fire Company (1796), helped incorporate the Borough of Bridgeport (1800), and founded the Bridgeport & Newtown Turnpike Company (1801). With William Peet he ran a saddlery business as Cannon & Peet.

With the tanner William Peet he ran the saddlery firm Cannon & Peet on Broad Street, then helped raise the subscription that bought Newfield its first fire engine in 1796 — fifteen dollars of his own among the names. He died in office as president of the Bridgeport Bank.

narrative_blocks.header: One enslaved person in his household
narrative_blocks.body: The 1790 census enumerated one enslaved person in his Norwalk household; later Bridgeport records name a man, Cato Cannon, among those he held. The turnpike-builder and bank president was, like many Connecticut founders of his day, a slaveholder.

cross_connections.display_label: shares a Peet family tie worth tracing

burialCemetery.name: Norwalk Union Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Norwalk Fairfield County CT. Burial site of the Lockwood-St. John families of this line.
